content: Hi @Asahi Lina ! I'm trying to access a resv field from a drm_gem_object, like here. I used the lookup_object binding and got an ObjectRef back. How can I access the resv field from the ObjectRef? Sorry for the question, I got a bit confused by the layers of abstractions :)

There's no abstraction for that yet, so you'd have to add it to the BaseObject trait in gem/mod.rs (there's a Deref along the way so whatever you add there should work directly on the ObjectRef). I have to say the GEM abstraction right now is a little bit... messy, I'm pretty sure it can be made less confusing ^^;;
Hi again @Asahi Lina ! Just another question about the gem abstractions. I'm trying to implement a personalized callback for gem_create_object. Is it possible to do it so in the current bindings?

Another question: Do you have any ideas for IOCTLs that don't start in 0x00? I'm working on a driver whose first IOCTL has the number 0x01 and the macro declare_drm_ioctls! is turning it into IOCTL 0x00.

@Maíra Canal That's what the DriverObject::new() callback is intended for (creates the driver-specific data for a GEM object). There is no way to replace the entire GEM object creation flow though, if that's what you want... I'm not sure how you would do that safely. Does the new() function work for you?

Asahi Lina said:

Maíra Canal That's what the DriverObject::new() callback is intended for (creates the driver-specific data for a GEM object). There is no way to replace the entire GEM object creation flow though, if that's what you want... I'm not sure how you would do that safely. Does the new() function work for you?

I'm not sure if the new() function works for me because I need to change a field inside the struct drm_gem_shmem_object (https://cgit.freedesktop.org/drm/drm-misc/tree/drivers/gpu/drm/vgem/vgem_drv.c#n106).
For the ioctl thing, I think you'll have to add a dummy at 0... I didn't even realize having holes in the ioctl list like that is legal, but it looks like the DRM code handles it...
Feel free to try to figure out how to improve the abstraction to make that more ergonomic ^^;; (I think the macro will have to get even messier but it's possible...)

I'm trying to figure out how vgem even creates objects and I think it goes through dumb_create? So we would have to either allow overriding that (which would let you set the field there instead of in the new callback), or add another callback that can mutate the already created GEM object after it is created, still in the gem_create_object callback.

Asahi Lina said:

I'm trying to figure out how vgem even creates objects and I think it goes through dumb_create? So we would have to either allow overriding that (which would let you set the field there instead of in the new callback), or add another callback that can mutate the already created GEM object after it is created, still in the gem_create_object callback.

I believe that maybe AllocImpl shouldn't be an internal implementation detail as many drivers have to modify the gem operations. I guess it would be nice to allow overring these callbacks. I will try to work on something like this.
Keep in mind the abstractions right now are basically "what I needed", they are not complete and probably never will be. The idea is we add things as we need them ^^

Hi folks! I was wondering if you guys could help me out with a Drop problem that I'm having. I'm initializing creating a new Registration struct for my driver in the init function (https://github.com/mairacanal/linux/blob/6c8eda039cfb3d21d6abfc753cf14c1b449af791/drivers/gpu/drm/rust-gem/vgem.rs#L65), but this Registration struct is not being dropped in the exit function and I cannot add it to the Vgem struct, as it's ownership is passed to kernel::new_device_data!. Do you guys have any suggestions on how to make the Registration struct be dropped?

Maíra Canal said:

[...] and I cannot add it to the Vgem struct, as it's ownership is passed to kernel::new_device_data!. Do you guys have any suggestions on how to make the Registration struct be dropped?

data.registrations() gives you the registrations. And data is an Arc<> so you should probably keep a handle to that data and get the registration from there? I do not fully understand the Drop-related function though... I think your subsystem wrapper should care about revoking your registrations? :thinking:

Erik Schilling said:

Maíra Canal said:

[...] and I cannot add it to the Vgem struct, as it's ownership is passed to kernel::new_device_data!. Do you guys have any suggestions on how to make the Registration struct be dropped?

data.registrations() gives you the registrations. And data is an Arc<> so you should probably keep a handle to that data and get the registration from there? I do not fully understand the Drop-related function though... I think your subsystem wrapper should care about revoking your registrations? :thinking:

I guess the subsystem should handle it, but for some reason drm::drv::Registration::drop() is not being called :neutral:
Just looked at your code again and realized that you are building a module that it not tied to a specific bus-subsystem. So you probably want to just revoke the registrations yourself on removal.

Erik Schilling said:

Just looked at your code again and realized that you are building a module that it not tied to a specific bus-subsystem. So you probably want to just revoke the registrations yourself on removal.

Thanks for the help! I called data.device_remove() inside the exit method and it worked!
